claim_reviews.claim: Every continued–fraction path with partial quotients in {1,…,5} corresponds to the 2×2 integer matrix product γ = g_{a1}⋯g_{ak} with g_a = [[a,1],[1,0]], and the denominator is γ_{2,1}.
claim_reviews.evidence: This is the standard correspondence between SL(2,ℤ) and regular continued fractions, used for example in Series (1985) and in Bourgain–Kontorovich (2014).

claim_reviews.claim: Reformulating the enumeration as batched 2×2 matrix multiplication on GPU removes all CPU bottlenecks and yields a 175× speed-up: 10^8 denominators are verified in 7.5 s on one NVIDIA B200 versus 157 s on the prior CPU tree-walk implementation.
claim_reviews.evidence: The stated time is plausible given the B200’s projected >100 TFLOP/s INT32 throughput and the ~10^9–10^10 integer multiplies required, yet no profiler output, code version hash, or baseline CPU specification is supplied, so the magnitude of the improvement cannot be independently reproduced from the report alone.
claim_reviews.concerns: Specify exact GPU clock, compiler flags, baseline CPU model, thread count and profiling logs.

claim_reviews.claim: An 8-GPU DGX B200 system verifies all denominators ≤10^10 in 43 s.
claim_reviews.evidence: Scaling by a factor of ~5.7× relative to the single-GPU 10^9 run is credible for embarrassingly parallel workloads, but the authors provide no strong-scaling data or confirmation that I/O and atomic writes do not throttle performance.
claim_reviews.concerns: Provide per-GPU timing, inter-GPU communication pattern, and peak memory usage.

claim_reviews.claim: Live matrix count peaks around depth 12–13 at ≈244 M matrices, requiring <8 GB of GPU memory.
claim_reviews.evidence: 244 M × 4 × 8 bytes ≈ 7.8 GB—consistent with the reported memory footprint and with known growth rates of restricted-digit CF trees under a 10^10 denominator cap.

claim_reviews.claim: The fused expand+mark+compact CUDA kernel with atomicAdd/atomicOr is the critical optimisation enabling the reported throughput.
claim_reviews.evidence: Kernel fusion and atomics are well-known optimisation techniques (cf. Merrill & Grimshaw, GPU Technology Conf. 2011), but the manuscript lacks kernel source or occupancy metrics demonstrating that fusion, rather than other factors (e.g., ILP or cache locality), is responsible for the speed-up.
claim_reviews.concerns: Include kernel listing, occupancy, and an ablation study separating fusion from other changes.

claim_reviews.claim: The speed-up factors in the table (175× at 10^7, ~1 000× at 10^8, ~10 000× at 10^9) faithfully represent performance gains over the previous v4 code base.
claim_reviews.evidence: No timings for v4 beyond 10^7 are shown; descriptors like “~hours” or “~days” are too coarse for verification, and the v4 source/compilation details are absent.
claim_reviews.concerns: Release v4 benchmark results with identical hardware and compiler toolchain.

overall_assessment: The mathematical foundation (CF–matrix equivalence) is sound and the GPU algorithm is conceptually correct. Reported runtimes are plausible for Blackwell-class hardware, but the manuscript lacks sufficient experimental detail to reproduce the headline 175×–10 000× speed-ups. With clearer benchmarking methodology and code artefacts, the result would be fully verifiable.
recommendations: Publish exact git commit hashes for v4 and v5 code paths
Provide full hardware specs (CPU model, GPU clocks, driver/CUDA versions)
Release raw timing logs and nvprof/nsys traces for key runs
Add an ablation study isolating the impact of kernel fusion
Clarify denominator uniqueness handling to exclude double counting
certification_justification: The computational approach builds directly on established continued-fraction/matrix theory and relies on standard GPU optimisation strategies documented in the literature, but no prior paper reports this specific enumeration technique.
